explanation: The procedure sets NOCOUNT OFF, causing row-count messages to be sent to clients.
XACT_ABORT is disabled even though the procedure uses explicit transactions, allowing some errors to leave partial work.
SELECT * returns every Sales.Orders column and makes callers sensitive to schema changes.
TOP (10) has no ORDER BY, so the selected orders are arbitrary.
Sales.Orders.OrderID is a primary key and already unique, making DISTINCT unnecessary.
NOLOCK on Sales.Orders permits dirty, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR is applied directly to OrderDate in the predicate, preventing a simple date-range access predicate.
The int CustomerID column is compared with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText, requiring an implicit conversion and risking conversion errors.
The @CustomerID IS NULL OR CustomerID = @CustomerID pattern impairs cardinality estimation and index selection.
The pattern N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard and cannot use a normal seek on Comments.
ISNULL wraps Sales.Orders.CustomerID in the predicate, preventing direct index matching.
The sentinel value 0 is embedded directly in the CustomerID predicate.
Customer identifier 1 is embedded directly in the predicate.
Adding zero to OrderID in the predicate prevents direct matching against the indexed key.
The CustomerID <> 1 predicate can require scanning a broad portion of Sales.Orders.
Customer identifier 1 is embedded directly in the predicate.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned Orders/Customers pair is arbitrary.
The CROSS JOIN multiplies every Sales.Orders row by every Sales.Customers row.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned order is arbitrary.
Sales.Orders and Sales.OrderLines are comma-separated and joined through WHERE instead of explicit JOIN syntax.
The OrderLines count is evaluated as a correlated scalar subquery for each outer order.
The EXISTS subquery performs a correlated OrderLines check for each Sales.Orders row.
The quantity threshold 1 is embedded directly in the OrderLines predicate.
The same Comments CASE expression is repeated in both the SELECT list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 depends on the select-list position and can silently change meaning when the list is edited.
Pagination orders only by non-unique OrderDate and lacks a stable key such as OrderID as a tie-breaker.
The OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 branches are disjoint, so UNION performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
Order identifier 1 is embedded directly in the first UNION branch.
Order identifier 2 is embedded directly in the second UNION branch.
COUNT(*) scans all matching OrderLines even though the predicate only needs to know whether one exists.
The correlated OrderLines count is calculated separately for each outer order.
SELECT assignment to @OrderID can consume multiple rows and silently retain an arbitrary last value.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so arbitrary OrderID values are inserted into @OrderIds.
SELECT INTO creates the #ImplicitOrders schema implicitly.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so #ImplicitOrders receives arbitrary orders.
order_cursor introduces row-by-row processing that should be expressed as a set operation.
The cursor's TOP (2) query has no ORDER BY, so it selects arbitrary orders.
The WHILE loop performs iterative work instead of a set-based operation.
The dynamic statement is assembled by concatenating @CustomerID into executable SQL text.
EXEC executes the assembled @Sql string without bound parameters.
The dynamic statement is assembled by concatenating @OrderID into executable SQL text.
sys.sp_executesql executes @Sql without a parameter declaration or bound parameter values.
Explicitly created temporary table #Scratch is not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and does not THROW, raise an error, or return failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path instead of THROW.
PRINT provides buffered, unstructured diagnostic output.
Customer identifier 1 is embedded directly in the Sales.Orders predicate.
The date constant '20160101' is embedded directly in the OrderDate predicate.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits a style, so the result depends on session date settings.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned joined row is arbitrary.
COALESCE wraps Sales.Orders.CustomerID in the join predicate and inhibits direct index matching.
COALESCE wraps Sales.Invoices.CustomerID in the join predicate and inhibits direct index matching.
The sentinel value 0 is embedded in the COALESCE expression for Orders.CustomerID.
The sentinel value 0 is embedded in the COALESCE expression for Invoices.CustomerID.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned order is arbitrary.
Sales.Orders is joined to itself on its primary key only to retrieve the same OrderID already present.
The query sorts by Comments, an nvarchar(max) column that can require a large and expensive sort.
SUM aggregates the entire fact-like Sales.OrderLines table without a restrictive filter.
The non-aggregate OrderID > 0 predicate is placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
COUNT(*) without GROUP BY returns one row, making ORDER BY OrderCount redundant.
MERGE is used for Sales.Orders and carries known concurrency and correctness risks.
The MERGE update assigns target.Comments to itself, producing logging and locking without changing data.
The UPDATE assigns Comments to its current value and performs a write without changing data.
Sales.OrderLines rows are deleted using the broad Quantity = -1 predicate rather than a stable key.
The sentinel quantity -1 is embedded directly in the DELETE predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a SQL Server worker for one second.
The explicit transaction begins outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
IDENTITY_INSERT is enabled for Sales.Orders, introducing fragile session-state coupling.
sp_who is invoked without the sys schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT changes session behavior and is not explicitly restored before the procedure ends.
The INSERT into #DemoCategories uses VALUES without an explicit target column list.
Category identifier 99 is embedded directly in the inserted values.
The constructed CustomerName pattern begins with %, preventing a normal index seek.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated into executable SQL text.
The nvarchar parameter @CustomerName is inserted directly into dynamic SQL and can alter the generated statement.
The generated dynamic LIKE pattern starts with %, preventing a normal CustomerName index seek.
sp_executesql executes @sql without declaring or binding parameters.
sp_executesql is invoked without the sys schema qualifier.
The UPDATE has no WHERE clause and changes CustomerCategoryID for every row in #DemoCustomers.
Customer category identifier 1 is embedded directly in the UPDATE.
Filtering o.OrderDate in WHERE rejects NULL-extended rows and effectively converts the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
The date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in the OrderDate filter.
The implicitly converted hyphenated date string has no explicit conversion style and can be session-sensitive.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL always evaluates to UNKNOWN; IS NULL is required.
dbo.GetCustomerType is invoked in the SELECT list and may execute once for every Sales.Customers row.
The transaction CATCH block only selects the error message and does not rethrow or report failure.
The CATCH block for the explicit transaction does not roll back an active or uncommittable transaction.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes a physical index choice for Sales.Customers.
The query contains three nested derived-table levels and should be simplified or refactored.
The pattern '%A%' starts with a wildcard and cannot use a normal CustomerName index seek.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples the result to every column returned by the function.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is a multi-statement table-valued function, which typically receives poorer optimization than an inline TVF.
